we'd all use super fresh oil in our soap recipes,

but I know that's not the reality for many of 
us. And I'd rather make the most of what I have

rather than waste precious resources. Whether I'm 
using the skin of the lemons or just the juice,

I always wash my fruit before processing it. 
Citrus fruit can hang on the tree for a long

time before it's harvested, so it's good to 
clean them well with a good scrub under fresh

water. I thought I might use some of the zest of 
these lemons in my soap, so I grated some of it,

but you'll see that later I didn't end up using 
it, and I'll explain why not when we get to that

part. The most important thing I needed to extract 
from these lemons was their juice. And wow, my

lemons were so ripe and soft. They were absolutely 
full of juice. If you've got hard unripe lemons,

just leave them out for a week or two and let them 
ripen. You'll know they're ripe when they've got

a bit of softness to them. You'll get a lot more 
juice out of them that way, and they'll also have

more sugars in them. If you're using Meyer lemons 
like these, their juice is about 4% citric acid by

weight. So, you'll need 315 grams of Meyer lemon 
juice for this recipe to achieve 1% citric acid of

the base soap oils used in the recipe. If you're 
using regular lemon varieties, not meyer lemons,

they have more citric acid in them. So, you'll 
need only 250ml/250g of fresh lemon juice. Don't

worry though, I'll put all of these little 
details in the written post on my website.

This recipe only applies to fresh lemon juice, 
not store-bought bottled juice. And it's really

important to know that when you add citric acid to 
soap recipes, the lye amount needs adjusting. So,

please do your homework and calculate a new 
recipe for yourself, unless you have meyer

lemons and use the same oils that I used in this 
recipe. Once you have your lemon juice squeezed,

strain out any pulp and measure 315 ml or 315 g 
of the juice into a jug. We need a total of 355

g of liquid for this recipe, but only 315 g of 
juice to get the right citric acid amount. So,

we need to add 40 g of water to bring the total 
liquid amount to 355 g. If you're using non Meyer

lemon type lemon juice, you need 250 g of fresh 
juice with 105 g of water to make the 355 g total.

Just add the water to the juice and stir them to 
combine. Then pop that into an ice cube tray and

freeze it for a few hours until it's solid. We'll 
be using this iced juice mixture to make the lye

solution for the soap. Next, I start preparing my 
other ingredients. I'm still trying to use up my

stash of palm oil. So, I'm using 441 g of that in 
this recipe. Please don't berate me for using palm

oil. I've got my reasons and I've made videos 
about it to explain that if you're interested.

I'm also just trying to use it up. So, waste not 
want not. You can also use tallow or lard in place

of palm oil if you have those available to you. 
They will work great in this recipe. In with the

palm oil, I add 315 gram of coconut oil, which I 
had sitting out in the sun to melt because it's

quite cool here. Palm fruit and coconut oils are 
the two hard fats I'm using in the recipe. So,

I put them together into one jug and very, very 
gently melted them together in the microwave.

I know there are a lot of microwave haters out 
there, but I honestly cannot find any evidence

whatsoever to suggest that there's anything wrong 
with doing this. Just use a low power setting. Go

really slow and low heat and stir it frequently 
as you go and it will just be a lovely gentle

melting method. While those are melting together, 
I measure my liquid oils. And for this recipe,

I'm using a simple combination of canola and 
castor oil. For the canola, we'll need 441 g,

which is the same amount as the palm oil. And 
for castor oil, we need only 63 g. Next, I put

my gloves and my safety goggles on and weighed out 
the sodium hydroxide, which will become the lye

solution that turns my oils into soap. Please know 
what you're doing when handling this ingredient.

It's highly corrosive and you really must protect 
your eyes and your skin at all times when making

soap from scratch. Carefully weigh 185 gram of 
sodium hydroxide into a container. Put the lid

on it straight away and place it somewhere secure 
until you're ready to make the soap. This sodium

hydroxide amount has been adjusted to account for 
the citric acid in my lemon juice which consumes

some of the lye. You'll notice in the bottom left 
corner of the screen that I've got a little bowl

of dried calendula flowers there. I didn't end 
up using those, so please ignore that. Next,

I prepared my essential oils, which are totally 
optional, but I wanted to add some to this recipe.

For a fresh lemony scent, I used 9 g of lemon 
myrtle essential oil, 10 g of litsea cubea,

and 12 g of cedar atlas essential oil. The lemon 
juice doesn't give any fragrance to this soap and

lemon essential oil isn't well suited for soap 
recipes. So, these are good alternatives that you

can use in small amounts or you can just leave the 
soap unscented. It will still be really beautiful.

Now that I have all of my ingredients ready, 
the last thing is to prepare my workstation,

get my molds ready, and wait for my lemon juice 
ice cubes to be fully frozen. I started off with a

beautiful sunny winter's day on this day, but then 
the clouds came over, so I lost my nice lighting,

but never mind. A bit of a extra work light saved 
the day. And once everything was ready to go,

it was time to make the soap. The first step is 
to put the lemon juice ice cubes into a jug to

make the lye solution. Making sure you've got 
your safety goggles and your gloves still on.

Carefully pour the sodium hydroxide granules over 
the ice and start mixing. The ice cubes will melt

as they start reacting with the lye and eventually 
you'll have a golden orange colored lemon juice

lye solution. It was such an amazing color that it 
was at this point where I decided not to use the

grated lemon zest or the calendula petals that I'd 
prepared. I really just wanted to see the color

of this soap without them added. And I'm glad I 
chose that option because they looked so nice in

the end. And yeah, it was just good to see what 
they'd be like just with the lemon juice. Once my

lye solution was mixed, I added my melted hard 
fats into my liquid fats or my liquid oils and

stirred them together. It's totally optional, but 
I decided to add in a little bit of white kaolin

clay. So, I put a heaped teaspoon of that into 
the oils and blended it through thoroughly. Then,

I did a quick temperature check and I was very 
happy to see that both my lye solution and my oils

were perfect at around 40° C, which is around 
about 102 F. You don't want your oils or your

lye solution to be too hot or your soap could 
thicken up too quickly. To mix the soap batter,

I carefully poured my lye solution into the oils 
using a strainer to filter out any small beads

of undissolved lye, which is really important 
when you're using ice for your lye solution. And

because of the palm oil and coconut oil in this 
recipe, along with the sugars in the lemon juice,

I blended this batch only for a couple of 
short bursts with my stick blender to begin

the emulsification and then I hand stirred it the 
rest of the way. If you're using individual molds,

you want to keep this batter as fluid as you can 
for as long as you can. So, try not to over blend

it. I kept a really close eye on this and as soon 
as I could feel it thickening, I quickly added the

essential oil, mixed that through thoroughly, 
and thankfully my molds were laid out on boards

ready to go. So, I grabbed them as I stirred and 
then poured the soap. I poured the soap batter

back into my large jug, and that made it a lot 
easier. Slowly and carefully, I poured the soap

batter into the molds. This batch made almost 18 
full flower soaps, which was perfect for my three

molds of six soaps each. I love this part of soap 
making. That moment when you realize you mixed

it just enough, but not too much that it got too 
thick to pour. You could absolutely make this soap

in log molds, too, which would be easier, but not 
as cute, I don't think. I really love these flower

soap molds. As many of you probably know. My soap 
was starting to thicken by the end of the pour,

but I just even the bars out as best as I could. 
I gave some of the thicker ones a little spiral

swirl with my chopstick to even out the lumps, and 
I think they came out pretty good. The final touch

was to spray these with alcohol, which helps 
reduce soda ash on the bars. Then I put them

into my slightly warm oven for a little while. I 
eventually took them out of the oven and put them

in my large cooler wrapped in a blanket overnight 
because I needed the oven. This often happens to

me, which is generally why I use the cooler. And 
this is where things got interesting. I'm so used

to making simple soaps in log molds that saponify 
quite quickly, usually within the first day or

so. So, I was a bit dismayed when I took these 
out a day later and discovered that they were

still really caustic and hadn't yet saponified. I 
freaked out a bit to be honest since I'd spent the

whole day the day before calculating, preparing, 
and filming the recipe. I honestly thought I'd

made a mistake in my calculations or maybe my 
lemon juice had a lot less citric acid in it than

I thought. So, I was a bit disappointed. But then 
I thought for a bit and realized that it might be

okay. It might just need more time. And that's 
what turned out to be the case. It was probably

the fact that it's winter here. The soap batter 
was not very warm and not very well insulated in

the individual molds, which can slow down the 
saponification process. And you know, perhaps

the citric acid slows things down a bit too, even 
though I did adjust the lye amount. I'm not sure,

but some soap recipes do take longer to saponify, 
particularly in individual molds where there's not

much gelling happening. I felt a bit more hopeful 
after taking out one of the bars on the first day,

and I hot processed it in the microwave, which 
is a nice quick way to see if the soap recipe

is okay or not. Thankfully, that test bar did 
completely saponify after I hot processed it. So,

I felt confident that the rest of the batch 
would be okay in a couple of days time. And

here is the soap 3 days later on Monday morning. 
All of the bars were smooth and fully saponified,

and they tested perfect with my pH test strips. 
So, it worked. The only caveat I'd say about this

recipe now is that you never really know how much 
citric acid exactly is in your lemon juice. So,

it's a bit of a gamble. But I think if you 
calculate a decent super fat into your recipe,

at least 5%, maybe even up to 8 or 9%, I 
did 5%. Then you should be okay. Either way,

I am very happy with this soap. It smells great. 
It looks wonderful. It helped me to use up some

more of my old palm oil and canola oils that I've 
been trying to use up bit by bit in each new video

recipe that I share. And it's a lovely soap with 
a rather nice lather, too. I used the little hot

processed bar to test the lather. And yeah, it's a 
really, really nice soap. I love the way it feels.

Have you tried lemon juice in your soap recipes 
or other citrus juices? How did you calculate

them? How did they go? I'd love to hear from you 
in the comments if you want to chat with me there.

And don't forget to see the description for all 
of the resources you will need for this recipe.

Have a brilliant day. Thanks for all of 
your support and I'll see you again soon.

videos.title: How to clarify lemon juice | Clarified Penicillin and Aviation

transcript.fullText:
Hi, friends of cocktails, and lovebirds. Today we’ll make a pair of cocktails, 
perfect for Valentine’s Day. I know that’s more than a week away, 
but this gives you time, if you need to get some of the ingredients. Most of us won’t be going out for dinner
on Valentine’s day, or anywhere else for that matter… So I’ll show you how to make something special
for yourself and your significant other. We’ll make my versions of two well-known cocktails, the Penicillin, and the Aviation. They look different, right? That’s because we’ll clarify
some lemon juice today. But before we start these cocktails may be called Mr. & Ms., but you can of course make, and drink them, as you wish. I just want you all to try something new. Let’s start, it’s Cocktail time! I first made these cocktails as a part of
a Valentine’s Day menu at the bar I worked at. You still remember that, right? Bars? Work? Good times… But what good are cocktails
if nobody can drink them? So here’s how you make them at home. For "Mr." cocktail you’ll need... Scotch Whisky, I’m using Chivas Regal Extra,
since it’s an extra special occasion, Bénédictine, for a herbal addition to the cocktail, clarified lemon juice, we’ll get to that in a second and simple syrup. Last part is Islay Single Malt Scotch, 
that we’ll float on the top. Like I said, it’s a twist on a Penicillin. For the "Ms." cocktail we’ll stick to the
Aviation recipe a bit more. Gin, we’ll use Monkey47, 
premium gin from the Black Forest in Germany maraschino liqueur again some clarified lemon juice, and of course, Crème de Violette, 
for that floral note and the beautiful color. Before we start making these cocktails 
I’ll show you how to make clarified lemon juice. And for that you’ll need agar-agar. You can get it through the affiliate link
in the description below. We’ll start by squeezing 300 ml, or 10 oz,
of lemon juice. Use whatever technique you prefer, 
but I find this to be quite fast. Squeezing it like this also expresses 
the essential oils from the peels. Then we’ll measure out 75 ml, or 2.5 oz,
and use that to mix with agar. Or agar-agar, both names are used. This is a jelly-like substance, obtained from red algae, 
which makes it a great vegan alternative  to gelatin. Discovered in 17th century Japan, it is now used in
microbiology, cuisine and of course, mixology. We’ll add 0.2% in weight of agar
compared to the total amount of lemon juice. For us that’s 0.6 g. Make sure you don’t use more than that because
too much agar will give the juice a metallic taste. If you’re clarifying juices with a more subtle taste, 
like a mandarine, use even less, 0.1% of agar. And the reason we’re only using a quarter of the total liquid 
is that agar needs to boil to hydrate and we don’t want to heat up all of the juice. That could alter the flavor. If you’re using heat-sensitive liquids like
strawberry or cucumber juice, or any spirits, use 1 part water to mix in the agar then add
3 parts of your room-temp liquid of choice. Slowly bring it to a boil to give agar the
time to hydrate and dissolve. And mix constantly with a whisk. Once you see that agar is fully dissolved,
take it off the heat then slowly add the rest of the lemon juice, 
whisking constantly. If you did it the other way around, agar could
chill down too fast and form a gel before it was incorporated with everything. Transfer the mixture to a bowl and place in
an ice bath or a freezer to form a soft gel. That will happen in about an hour or two. Then comes the filtration. First we’ll lightly break it apart. Then take another bowl with a strainer and
a cheesecloth and pour the gel over. The agar will trap all the impurities and
you’ll end up with clarified lemon juice. This not only looks good but also makes for
lighter and brighter cocktails, it can be carbonized and it’s suitable for making
pre-batched cocktails. Since the filtration takes a long time and
this is still citrus juice, you can place it in the fridge and let it clarify there. You may be tempted to rush things and squeeze
the cheesecloth to speed up the process, but you’ll quickly end up with cloudy juice,
so be warned. Then all you need to do is bottle it 
and use it in your favorite cocktail. And unlike milk-washing, where you mix the cocktail first 
and then make it clear with the help of milk curdles, using clarified citrus juice doesn’t change the texture 
or soften the cocktail. Your eyes might be fooled, 
but your palate won’t be. We have one more thing to prepare 
before we start making the cocktails and that’s the garnish inside the rocks glass 
for Mr. cocktail. We’ll make a long strip of beetroot paste,
which will release subtle earthy aroma and flavors into the cocktail, pairing nicely
with the smokey scotch and the herbal benedictine. We already did a similar thing with chocolate
paint for a wine cocktail. But since beetroot won’t melt we’ll use beetroot juice 
and add pectin to make it into a paste. Pectin is a gelling agent mainly extracted from citrus fruits 
and used to thicken things like jams. As is often the case in mixology,
we’ll need to use just the right amount, so a precise kitchen scale is a good investment. We’re using 100 grams of beetroot juice
so we’ll need to add 1.8 grams of pectin. But to get it nicely incorporated we’ll
heat up the juice first, then blend both of the ingredients in a blender. If you want the beetroot to release its flavors
into your cocktail slower, add more pectin, which will also make the paste thicker. Adding less would dissolve it faster, but
might also make the beetroot strip to peel off the glass and float into your drink. Filtering the mixture through a cheesecloth
will catch any solid particles if you’re using a freshly juiced beetroot, but it will
also keep back all of the foam from the blending. We really don’t want the foam when brushing
the beetroot into the glass. Before you place this filtered mixture in
the fridge transfer it to a small jar, which you’ll be able to shake up 
before applying to the glass. Again, if you’re in a rush, 
just place it in an ice bath. While that was chilling we also placed a rocks
glass in the freezer. This will ensure that the beetroot paste will
stick to it when applied. The key is that you brush a thick strip from
the bottom to the top of the glass. The more you apply, the more earthy notes your cocktail 
will develop as it dissolves into the drink. After your work of art is done, place it back
in the freezer until the cocktail is ready. With that done we can finally start making the cocktails and if making both you’ll want to start with
Ms. cocktail, since we’ll serve it up and it won’t dilute in the glass. Oh, and we’ll be throwing both of cocktails. Start by chilling the small shaker tin since
we’ll be catching the ingredients in the larger tin, making it a bit easier. But this still requires some practice, so avoid carpets. The first thing we’ll add will be the batch
distilled and handcrafted gin, Monkey47. Of the exactly 47 plant ingredients in this Schwarzwald Dry Gin it’s the lingonberries from the Black Forest 
that give it its distinguishing flavor. This will be a great choice for the Ms. 
Add 45 ml or 1.5 oz. Next we’ll add Maraschino. 22.5 ml or 0.75 oz will give just enough cherry-flavored
dry-sweetness this cocktail needs. Then comes our special ingredient, 
clarified lemon juice. Just like maraschino, we’ll add 22.5 ml or 0.75 oz. Since there’s no pulp in this juice 
and we won’t shake the cocktail, there will be no need for double straining at the end. The refreshing balance of this gin-sour cocktail
will be enriched by the final ingredient, Since this one has a really strong floral
taste and an intense color, 2 barspoons will be enough. Since we’re using clarified lemon juice
there’s no need to shake the cocktail, which would agitate the ingredients, 
making them cloudy. But by throwing it we’ll still add more
aeration than we would with stirring. This will release additional aromatics from certain ingredients, 
like the gin and violette liqueur. Depending on the ice you use you’ll see the volume 
of the drink has increased after 5-6 throws and you’ll even see the drink
has chilled the bottom shaker. That’s when your significant other helps
you out with a chilled glass and you’re ready to pour this beautiful cocktail. Garnish it with a pansy flower and set it aside while you make the Mr. cocktail. We’re using the same technique so again chill the small shaker 
tin and let’s add the ingredients into the large tin. To learn the throwing technique I first practiced
with water. The base of the Mr. cocktail will be a special
edition of Chivas scotch, which has been aged for 13 years and 
selectively matured in Oloroso sherry casks. This adds an extra layer of rich and intense
flavor and aroma. You need something like this to stand up to
the strong flavors in this cocktail. So add 45 ml or 1.5 oz. Instead of the honey-ginger syrup of the penicillin
cocktail we’ll add 15 ml or 0.5 oz of Bénédictine. This honey-sweetened herbal liqueur 
will make our version even better. But to balance the cocktail we'll need to add
some more sweetness. 1 barspoon of simple syrup will be perfect. And the last ingredient to go into the tin,
the clarified lemon juice. If you’ve got leftover clarified lemon juice
that you won’t use, you can freeze it and use it another time. Throwing techniques vary just like shaking. You can start slow and go from there. Focus on the falling liquid and your catching hand. There’s no need to twist when you start out. First find that balance of not pouring too fast 
or lowering your catching hand too fast. The higher the liquid falls the better the aeration. Properly throwing the cocktail also changes
its viscosity and gives it a different mouthfeel than shaking or stirring. Before we pour this into our garnished glass
we’ll add a large clear ice cube, being careful not to touch the beetroot paint. Then slowly pour the cocktail away from the garnish. Already looks great, but let’s finish it
with a barspon of peated scotch as a float. This isn’t as visible as a wine float, but
trust me, you’ll know it’s there. And now, the celebration of love 
and affection can begin. Cheers, my love! The smoky and fresh aroma are very upfront with subtle herbal notes coming through as well. On the taste the smokiness is followed by
the pleasantly fruity and full-bodied Chivas Extra scotch. And while this looks like a spirit-forward
old-fashioned-like cocktail, your palate will be pleasantly surprised by the balance of
sour, herbal and sweet components in the background. All of this is enriched by the light texture
we got from throwing. As you’ll sip this drink the earthy notes
from the beetroot will add another dimension to this really interesting cocktail. So take your time and enjoy it. Or share it, that’s nice too. Uuu, a note. Aww… Check down in the comments bellow what she wrote. And let me know if you’re planning something
special for Valentine’s day! And now let’s try the Ms. Since this is basically a stylish Aviation the floral notes are still the star of the show. The botanicals from Monkey47 
work great with the violette liqueur and even though looks can be deceiving, our palate is rewarded 
with a delicious sour cocktail. The clarified lemon juice provides a brighter
cocktail you’d expect and the subtle fruity dryness of 
the maraschino adds some complexity. It’s a slight twist on the classic, just enough 
to make it perfect for a special occasion. That’s it for today, thank you so much for watching 
and remember to show each other love all year. But make this on Valentine's day. Cheers! [Phantom Hand] *khm khm!* That’s because we’ll clarify some lemon juice today. But before we start.
*hits the microphone arm* Dude... That’s because… Dude... Because we’ll clarify… [Director] Damn, now I moved my hand. You’re putting stuff on my record player. There better not be any scratches on the plastic. This is the end of the video. If you want to see more cocktail recipes,
and more bloopers, check these out. And think about subscribing. Thanks for watching!

segments.text: Hi, friends of cocktails, and lovebirds.

Today we’ll make a pair of cocktails, 
perfect for Valentine’s Day.

I know that’s more than a week away, 
but this gives you time,
if you need to get some of the ingredients.

Most of us won’t be going out for dinner
on Valentine’s day,
or anywhere else for that matter…

So I’ll show you how to make something special
for yourself and your significant other.
We’ll make my versions of two well-known cocktails,
the Penicillin, and the Aviation.
They look different, right?

That’s because we’ll clarify
some lemon juice today.
But before we start
these cocktails may be called Mr. & Ms.,
but you can of course make, and drink them, as you wish.
I just want you all to try something new.
Let’s start, it’s Cocktail time!

I first made these cocktails as a part of
a Valentine’s Day menu at the bar I worked at.
You still remember that, right?
Bars?
Work?
Good times…

But what good are cocktails
if nobody can drink them?
So here’s how you make them at home.
For "Mr." cocktail you’ll need...

Scotch Whisky, I’m using Chivas Regal Extra,
since it’s an extra special occasion,
Bénédictine, for a herbal addition to the cocktail,
clarified lemon juice, we’ll get to that in a second
and simple syrup.

Last part is Islay Single Malt Scotch, 
that we’ll float on the top.
Like I said, it’s a twist on a Penicillin.

For the "Ms." cocktail we’ll stick to the
Aviation recipe a bit more.

Gin, we’ll use Monkey47, 
premium gin from the Black Forest in Germany
maraschino liqueur
again some clarified lemon juice,

and of course, Crème de Violette, 
for that floral note and the beautiful color.

Before we start making these cocktails 
I’ll show you how to make clarified lemon juice.
And for that you’ll need agar-agar.

You can get it through the affiliate link
in the description below.

We’ll start by squeezing 300 ml, or 10 oz,
of lemon juice.

Use whatever technique you prefer, 
but I find this to be quite fast.

Squeezing it like this also expresses 
the essential oils from the peels.

Then we’ll measure out 75 ml, or 2.5 oz,
and use that to mix with agar.
Or agar-agar, both names are used.

This is a jelly-like substance, obtained from red algae, 
which makes it a great vegan alternative  to gelatin.

Discovered in 17th century Japan, it is now used in
microbiology, cuisine and of course, mixology.

We’ll add 0.2% in weight of agar
compared to the total amount of lemon juice.
For us that’s 0.6 g.

Make sure you don’t use more than that because
too much agar will give the juice a metallic taste.

If you’re clarifying juices with a more subtle taste, 
like a mandarine, use even less, 0.1% of agar.

And the reason we’re only using a quarter of the total liquid 
is that agar needs to boil to hydrate
and we don’t want to heat up all of the juice.
That could alter the flavor.

If you’re using heat-sensitive liquids like
strawberry or cucumber juice, or any spirits,

use 1 part water to mix in the agar then add
3 parts of your room-temp liquid of choice.

Slowly bring it to a boil to give agar the
time to hydrate and dissolve.
And mix constantly with a whisk.

Once you see that agar is fully dissolved,
take it off the heat

then slowly add the rest of the lemon juice, 
whisking constantly.

If you did it the other way around, agar could
chill down too fast and form a gel
before it was incorporated with everything.

Transfer the mixture to a bowl and place in
an ice bath or a freezer to form a soft gel.
That will happen in about an hour or two.
Then comes the filtration.
First we’ll lightly break it apart.

Then take another bowl with a strainer and
a cheesecloth and pour the gel over.

The agar will trap all the impurities and
you’ll end up with clarified lemon juice.

This not only looks good but also makes for
lighter and brighter cocktails,

it can be carbonized and it’s suitable for making
pre-batched cocktails.

Since the filtration takes a long time and
this is still citrus juice,
you can place it in the fridge and let it clarify there.

You may be tempted to rush things and squeeze
the cheesecloth to speed up the process,

but you’ll quickly end up with cloudy juice,
so be warned.

Then all you need to do is bottle it 
and use it in your favorite cocktail.

And unlike milk-washing, where you mix the cocktail first 
and then make it clear with the help of milk curdles,

using clarified citrus juice doesn’t change the texture 
or soften the cocktail.

Your eyes might be fooled, 
but your palate won’t be.

We have one more thing to prepare

and that’s the garnish inside the rocks glass 
for Mr. cocktail.

We’ll make a long strip of beetroot paste,
which will release subtle earthy aroma

and flavors into the cocktail, pairing nicely
with the smokey scotch and the herbal benedictine.

We already did a similar thing with chocolate
paint for a wine cocktail.

But since beetroot won’t melt we’ll use beetroot juice 
and add pectin to make it into a paste.

Pectin is a gelling agent mainly extracted from citrus fruits 
and used to thicken things like jams.

As is often the case in mixology,
we’ll need to use just the right amount,
so a precise kitchen scale is a good investment.

We’re using 100 grams of beetroot juice
so we’ll need to add 1.8 grams of pectin.

But to get it nicely incorporated we’ll
heat up the juice first,
then blend both of the ingredients in a blender.

If you want the beetroot to release its flavors
into your cocktail slower, add more pectin,
which will also make the paste thicker.

Adding less would dissolve it faster, but
might also make the beetroot strip to peel
off the glass and float into your drink.

Filtering the mixture through a cheesecloth
will catch any solid particles

if you’re using a freshly juiced beetroot, but it will
also keep back all of the foam from the blending.

We really don’t want the foam when brushing
the beetroot into the glass.

Before you place this filtered mixture in
the fridge transfer it to a small jar,

which you’ll be able to shake up 
before applying to the glass.

Again, if you’re in a rush, 
just place it in an ice bath.

While that was chilling we also placed a rocks
glass in the freezer.

This will ensure that the beetroot paste will
stick to it when applied.

The key is that you brush a thick strip from
the bottom to the top of the glass.

The more you apply, the more earthy notes your cocktail 
will develop as it dissolves into the drink.

After your work of art is done, place it back
in the freezer until the cocktail is ready.
With that done we can finally start making the cocktails

and if making both you’ll want to start with
Ms. cocktail, since we’ll serve it up
and it won’t dilute in the glass.
Oh, and we’ll be throwing both of cocktails.

Start by chilling the small shaker tin since
we’ll be catching the ingredients
in the larger tin, making it a bit easier.
But this still requires some practice, so avoid carpets.

The first thing we’ll add will be the batch
distilled and handcrafted gin, Monkey47.
Of the exactly 47 plant ingredients in this Schwarzwald Dry Gin

it’s the lingonberries from the Black Forest 
that give it its distinguishing flavor.

This will be a great choice for the Ms. 
Add 45 ml or 1.5 oz.
Next we’ll add Maraschino.

22.5 ml or 0.75 oz will give just enough cherry-flavored
dry-sweetness this cocktail needs.

Then comes our special ingredient, 
clarified lemon juice.
Just like maraschino, we’ll add 22.5 ml or 0.75 oz.

Since there’s no pulp in this juice 
and we won’t shake the cocktail,
there will be no need for double straining at the end.

The refreshing balance of this gin-sour cocktail
will be enriched by the final ingredient,

Since this one has a really strong floral
taste and an intense color,
2 barspoons will be enough.

Since we’re using clarified lemon juice
there’s no need to shake the cocktail,

which would agitate the ingredients, 
making them cloudy.

But by throwing it we’ll still add more
aeration than we would with stirring.

This will release additional aromatics from certain ingredients, 
like the gin and violette liqueur.

Depending on the ice you use you’ll see the volume 
of the drink has increased after 5-6 throws

and you’ll even see the drink
has chilled the bottom shaker.

That’s when your significant other helps
you out with a chilled glass
and you’re ready to pour this beautiful cocktail.
Garnish it with a pansy flower
and set it aside while you make the Mr. cocktail.

We’re using the same technique so again chill the small shaker 
tin and let’s add the ingredients into the large tin.

To learn the throwing technique I first practiced
with water.

The base of the Mr. cocktail will be a special
edition of Chivas scotch,

which has been aged for 13 years and 
selectively matured in Oloroso sherry casks.

This adds an extra layer of rich and intense
flavor and aroma.

You need something like this to stand up to
the strong flavors in this cocktail.
So add 45 ml or 1.5 oz.

Instead of the honey-ginger syrup of the penicillin
cocktail we’ll add 15 ml or 0.5 oz of Bénédictine.

This honey-sweetened herbal liqueur 
will make our version even better.

But to balance the cocktail we'll need to add
some more sweetness.
1 barspoon of simple syrup will be perfect.

And the last ingredient to go into the tin,
the clarified lemon juice.

If you’ve got leftover clarified lemon juice
that you won’t use, you can freeze it
and use it another time.
Throwing techniques vary just like shaking.
You can start slow and go from there.
Focus on the falling liquid and your catching hand.
There’s no need to twist when you start out.

First find that balance of not pouring too fast 
or lowering your catching hand too fast.
The higher the liquid falls the better the aeration.

Properly throwing the cocktail also changes
its viscosity and gives it a different mouthfeel
than shaking or stirring.

Before we pour this into our garnished glass
we’ll add a large clear ice cube,
being careful not to touch the beetroot paint.
Then slowly pour the cocktail away from the garnish.

Already looks great, but let’s finish it
with a barspon of peated scotch as a float.

This isn’t as visible as a wine float, but
trust me, you’ll know it’s there.

And now, the celebration of love 
and affection can begin.
Cheers, my love!
The smoky and fresh aroma are very upfront
with subtle herbal notes coming through as well.

On the taste the smokiness is followed by
the pleasantly fruity and full-bodied Chivas Extra scotch.

And while this looks like a spirit-forward
old-fashioned-like cocktail,

your palate will be pleasantly surprised by the balance of
sour, herbal and sweet components in the background.

All of this is enriched by the light texture
we got from throwing.

As you’ll sip this drink the earthy notes
from the beetroot will add another dimension
to this really interesting cocktail.
So take your time and enjoy it.
Or share it, that’s nice too.
Uuu, a note.
Aww…
Check down in the comments bellow what she wrote.

And let me know if you’re planning something
special for Valentine’s day!
And now let’s try the Ms.
Since this is basically a stylish Aviation
the floral notes are still the star of the show.

The botanicals from Monkey47 
work great with the violette liqueur

and even though looks can be deceiving, our palate is rewarded 
with a delicious sour cocktail.

The clarified lemon juice provides a brighter
cocktail you’d expect

and the subtle fruity dryness of 
the maraschino adds some complexity.

It’s a slight twist on the classic, just enough 
to make it perfect for a special occasion.

That’s it for today, thank you so much for watching 
and remember to show each other love all year.
But make this on Valentine's day.
Cheers!
[Phantom Hand] *khm khm!*
That’s because we’ll clarify some lemon juice today.

But before we start.
*hits the microphone arm*
Dude...
That’s because…
Dude...
Because we’ll clarify…
[Director] Damn, now I moved my hand.
You’re putting stuff on my record player.
There better not be any scratches on the plastic.
This is the end of the video.

If you want to see more cocktail recipes,
and more bloopers, check these out.
And think about subscribing.
Thanks for watching!

videos.title: Drink Lemon Water for 30 Days | Dr. Zee | डॉक्टर ज़ी

transcript.fullText: अगर आप हर रोज 30 दिन तक लींबू पानी पिए तो क्या होगा? हमारा सारा फोकस हाई ब्लड प्रेशर पे होता है। लेकिन कोई भी लो ब्लड प्रेशर की बात नहीं करता जिसमें आपके जिस्म में खून का वॉल्यूम ही कम होता है। सुबह-सुबह जब आप सोकर उठते हैं नींद से तो ये दिन का वो वक्त होता है जब आपके जिस्म में ब्लड का वॉल्यूम सबसे कम होता है। रात तक ये वॉल्यूम ज्यादा हो जाता है। जब आपका ब्लड वॉल्यूम कम होता है तो क्या होता है? आप जब अचानक खड़े होते हैं तो चक्कर आ सकते हैं। तो यह आपका ब्लड प्रेशर कम होने की निशानी हो सकती है। अगर हम एक एललीट एथलीट को लें और उसका मुकाबला एक आम बंदे से करें जो ज्यादा एक्सरसाइज नहीं करता तो एक एथलीट के जिस्म में 50% ज्यादा ब्लड वॉल्यूम होता है। तो डेफिनेटली आपको एक्सरसाइज करते वक्त वो सेम परफॉर्मेंस नहीं मिलेगी। अक्सर लोगों को सुबह उठकर कुछ भी पीना अच्छा नहीं लगता। सुबह के वक्त कोई भी लिक्विड पीते हैं तो अजीब सा फील होता है। दिल मत ललाता है। इसलिए सुबह उठ के लोग चाय या कॉफी पीना पसंद करते हैं। मैं आपको बताता हूं कि यह क्यों होता है और फिर मैं आपको इसका सशन भी दूंगा। अगर आप सिर्फ पानी पीते हैं वो भी सुबह-सुबह जब आपका ब्लड वॉल्यूम सबसे कम होता है तो आप बेसिकली अपने इलेक्ट्रोलाइट्स को डाइल्यूट कर रहे होते हैं। यह बहुत बड़ी गलती है क्योंकि आप फिर और ज्यादा डिहाइड्रेटेड फील करते हैं। इससे मजीद प्यास बढ़ती है। सिर्फ पानी से आपका ब्लड वॉल्यूम नहीं बढ़ता। आपको इसमें थोड़ा सा नमक भी चाहिए। यह नमक ही आपके जिस्म में ब्लड में पानी को होल्ड करने में मदद करता है। तो बजाय सादा पानी के एक चुटकी नमक लेमन के साथ पानी में मिलाकर पीने से आप ज्यादा अच्छा फील करेंगे और अगर आप यह 30 दिन तक रोज करें स्पेशली अगर आप एक्सरसाइज से एक घंटा पहले पिएं तो आपको अपनी परफॉर्मेंस में और अपनी डेली एनर्जी में एक वाज़ फर्क फील होगा। और फिर आप अगर 24 घंटों के सिरकेडियन रिदमम को देखें जो हमारा नेचुरल बायोलॉजिकल क्लॉक है तो सबसे ज्यादा कॉर्टिसोल लेवल यानी स्ट्रेस हार्मोन यह किस वक्त होता है? सुबह 8:00 बजे। सुबह के वक्त आपके जिस्म में कॉर्टिसोल का लेवल सबसे ज्यादा होता है। लेकिन क्या आप जानते हैं कि कॉर्टिसोल को नीचे लाने वाली एक बहुत ही पावरफुल चीज कौन सी है? सोडियम। जी हां, बिल्कुल सही सुना आपने। इसी वजह से कुछ लोग सोने से पहले जब हल्का सा साल्ट पानी में मिलाकर लेते हैं तो इससे इनका कॉर्टिसोल लेवल नीचे आता है। स्ट्रेस हॉर्मोन का लेवल नीचे आता है जिससे इनकी नींद बेहतर होती है। फिर सुबह जब कॉर्टिसोल का लेवल एकदम हाई होता है तो यह ना सिर्फ स्ट्रेस को बढ़ाता है बल्कि लिवर में स्टर्ड शुगर को भी बाहर निकालने लगता है और इसका डायरेक्ट असर ब्लड शुगर पे पड़ता है। तो यह चीज आपके इल्म में जरूर होनी चाहिए। वो यह है कि साल्ट कॉर्टिसोल को रिड्यूस कर सकता है। और अगर आप सुबह के वक्त थोड़ा सा साल्ट लें तो आप दिन की शुरुआत अच्छी कर सकते हैं। एक स्ट्रेस फ्री मोड में नॉर्मल फ्लूइड बैलेंस के साथ बेहतर एनर्जी जिससे आपको एक्सरसाइज में भी हेल्प मिलेगी और वो जो चक्कर आते हैं वो भी नहीं आएंगे जब आप अचानक उठकर खड़े होते हैं। यह मसला होगा ही नहीं जब आपका ब्लड वॉल्यूम पूरा होगा। तो यह फायदा है नमक का और कोशिश करें हिमालयन सी साल्ट लें। लेकिन अगर आपको हाई ब्लड प्रेशर की हिस्ट्री है तो आपको ज्यादा पोटाशियम और मैग्नीशियम की जरूरत है। तो इसमें सोडियम आपको कम करना है लेकिन बिल्कुल छोड़ना नहीं है। तो आप लो सोडियम साल्ट इस्तेमाल कर सकते हैं। मार्किट से आप किसी भी अच्छे ब्रांड का ले लें। कुछ में 60% सोडियम होता है। कुछ में 70% होता है और बाकी सारे मिनरल्स भी होते हैं। अब बात करते हैं लेमन के फवायद की। अगर आप फ्रेश लेमन यूज़ करते हैं। एक लेमन को दो हिस्सों में काट लें। दोनों हिस्सों को अच्छे से निचोड़ लें तो आपके पास आ जाएगा प्योर लेमन जूस। फ्रेश लेमन जूस से जो चीजें आपको मिलेंगी वो बॉटल वाले लेमन जूस में नहीं होती। जैसे विटामिन सी और विटामिन सी आपकी स्किन के लिए बहुत अच्छा है। ये ना सिर्फ स्किन के लिए अच्छा है बल्कि आपकी आर्टरीज के लिए एक बेहतरीन एंटीऑक्सीडेंट भी है। और लेमन का एक और पावरफुल कॉम्पोनेंट होता है सिट्रिक एसिड। यह सिट्रिक एसिड आपके गुर्दों को प्रोटेक्ट करता है। किडनी स्टोंस को बनने से रोकता है। यह जिस्म में जाने के बाद ऑक्सिलेट्स के साथ बाइंड कर लेता है। जो किडनी स्टोंस की मेन वजह होते हैं। और लेमन का यही एसिड यूरिक एसिड को भी कम करता है। तो लेमन गाउट वालों के लिए भी फायदेमंद है और यह गुर्दों के लिए भी बहुत ही बेहतरीन चीज है। लेमन के कुछ खास फाइटोनट्रिएंट्स आपके लिवर को डिटॉक्स करने में भी हेल्प करते हैं। फेज वन और फेज टू डिटॉक्सिफिकेशन को एक्टिवेट करते हैं। जिससे जिस्म में फंसे हुए हर तरह के केमिकल्स को लिवर बाहर निकालने लगता है। लेमन आपके बाइल को भी रेगुलेट करता है। यह खट्टा होता है तो इसकी खटास बाइल को पतला करने में हेल्प करती है। जिससे गॉलस्टोंस का रिस्क कम होता है और लिवर का डिटॉक्स और भी स्ट्रांग हो जाता है। लेमन और साल्ट का कॉम्बिनेशन आपके स्टमक के लिए भी बहुत पावरफुल है। यह आपके डाइजेस्टिव एंजाइम्स और जूसेस को एक्टिव करता है जिससे हाजमे का निजाम तेज होता है। लेमन की कुछ प्रॉपर्टीज कोलेस्ट्रॉल को भी रेगुलेट करने में मदद करती हैं। लेमन में मौजूद कुछ एंटीऑक्सीडेंट्स डायरेक्ट आर्टरीज के अंदर जाकर सफाई करते हैं। जिससे दिल की बीमारियों का रिस्क कम हो जाता है। और सबसे इंटरेस्टिंग बात लेमन में जो पॉलीफिनोल्स हैं ये आपके गट माइक्रोबायोम की डायवर्सिटी को बढ़ाते हैं। यानी पेट के अंदर मौजूद फ्रेंडली जरासीमों को एक्टिव और हेल्दी बनाते हैं। इनकी ग्रोथ को सपोर्ट करते हैं। लेकिन एक बात का बहुत ध्यान रखें अगर आप यह लेमन वाटर रोज पीने वाले हैं तो इसकी एसिडिटी आपके दांतों के एनामल को डैमेज कर सकती है क्योंकि इसमें एसिड होता है। यह दांतों की ऊपरी परत को खराब कर सकता है। तो कोशिश करें आप यह ड्रिंक स्ट्रॉ से पिएं और पीने के बाद मुंह को सादे पानी से रन्स कर लें, कुल्ली कर लें ताकि एसिड दांतों को नुकसान ना पहुंचाए। मैं खुद भी ये लेमन वाटर सुबह के वक्त लेता हूं और कभी कबभार मैं एक पूरा नींबू छिलके समेत बीजों समेत इसका जूस निकाल लेता हूं। थोड़ी सी अदरक डालता हूं। थोड़ा सा सेब डालता हूं इसकी कड़वाहट कम करने के लिए क्योंकि यह ड्रिंक बहुत ज्यादा खट्टा और कड़वा होता है। और इसमें एक गिलास पानी डाल लेता हूं। तो इस तरह यह एक बेहतरीन डिटॉक्स लेमनेड आपका बन जाता है और इसका टेस्ट इतना अच्छा होता है कि आपके बच्चे भी इसे मजे से पिएंगे। कुछ लोग तो इस मिक्सचर में थोड़ा सा एप्पल साइडर विनेगर भी डाल लेते हैं। यह भी बेहतरीन है। अगर आप इस ड्रिंक को नेक्स्ट लेवल पर ले जाना चाहते हैं तो एक टेबलस्पून एसी भी ट्राई कर सकते हैं। तो यह थी एक सिंपल सी क्विक टिप जो शायद आपकी हेल्थ के लिए गेम चेंजर साबित हो सकती है। और यह थी आज की वीडियो। मैं आपसे मिलता हूं नेक्स्ट वीडियो में। अपना ख्याल रखिए। अल्लाह हाफिज़।

videos.title: Here’s How I Store LEMONS for 1 YEAR Without Spoiling | Annie Tips

transcript.fullText:
You guys, I'm so excited to share this with you. 
This is how you can keep lemons fresh for an entire year. Just look at this jar I preserved 
these last August, and now it's September, which makes it a full year. And yet, the lemons 
are still juicy, plump, and bright like they're brand new. I even cut one open, and it was 
full of fresh lemon juice. And the best part, I'll also show you a few other storage methods. 
You can pick whichever one works best for you because all five of them really do work. Let's get 
started. After bringing your lemons home, the very first thing you should do is prepare a basin of 
clean water. Then, gently place the lemons inside. Use your hands to lightly rub the 
outer skin to remove any dirt. This step is essential because the 
peel is often coated with a thin layer of natural wax that needs to 
be cleaned off before storing. Once you've given them a quick rinse, take the 
lemons out and move on to a deeper cleaning. Prepare a bowl of warm water about 86° F, 30°. Add 1 tbsp of baking soda and stir 
until it's completely dissolved. Place the lemons in the solution and let 
them soak for about 7 to 10 minutes. This method thoroughly cleans the peel and helps 
remove any impurities or pesticide residue. After soaking, pour out the mixture and rinse 
the lemons again with clean water. By the way, where are you watching this video from? 
Leave me a comment below. I truly enjoy knowing that we can connect from all around the 
world. When the lemons are completely clean, lift them out into a basket and set them in a well 
ventilated spot so the surface can air dry fully. Now, prepare a clean glass jar with a tight 
fitting lid sized appropriately for the number of lemons you want to store. Gently arrange 
the lemons inside, either standing upright or lying sideways, depending on the jar's shape, 
to save space. Be careful not to pack them too tightly or bump them against one another. 
Since scratches or bruises on the skin can affect both quality and shelf life. Next, 
take a large bowl, pour in about one quart, one liter of clean water, then add 2 tablesp 
of salt and stir until it dissolves completely. Pour this brine into the jar until 
all the lemons are fully submerged. Finally, seal the jar tightly and keep it in 
a cool, airy place away from direct sunlight. With this method, your lemons will stay fresh, 
juicy, and flavorful for two to three months. Give it a try. You'll be pleasantly surprised 
by how well it works. Tip number two helps keep lemons fresh without using water. First, make sure 
your lemons are thoroughly washed and completely dried to prevent mold during storage. Next, 
prepare a piece of plastic wrap large enough to cover one lemon. Place the lemon in the 
center of the wrap. Then, gently roll it up, pressing the wrap tightly against the peel 
to minimize any air from getting inside. Repeat this step with the rest of the lemons. Once all the lemons are wrapped, neatly 
arrange them in a food grade plastic container. Close the lid and place the container 
in the refrigerator's crisper drawer, the ideal spot with stable humidity and 
temperature to keep lemons fresh. This method keeps lemons juicy and fresh for about 2 
3 weeks. Plastic wrap also prevents the peel from drying out, shriveling, or turning bitter 
from prolonged air exposure. In addition, you can also try tip number three to 
keep lemons fresh for much longer. First, prepare a clean cutting board and place a lemon on 
it. Use a knife to make an X-shaped cut on the top of the lemon, but be careful not to cut all the 
way through. The bottom should still stay intact. Repeat this step with the rest of your lemons. After scoring, sprinkle about 1 tsp 
of salt into the slits of each lemon. Next, place the lemons into a clean, dry glass 
jar. As you add each lemon, press down lightly so that some of the salt falls to the bottom of the 
jar. This helps the salt distribute more evenly, which supports a faster and more consistent 
fermentation process. Continue layering until the jar is nearly full. At this point, you can 
add a few whole black pepperc corns or slices of fresh ginger to enhance the flavor, making 
the preserved lemons more fragrant and rich. Finally, pour in filtered water until the lemons 
are fully submerged. Then, seal the jar tightly. Leave the jar at room temperature for 3 4 weeks 
to ferment, shaking or turning it occasionally to redistribute the salt. After fermentation, store 
the jar in the refrigerator. The preserved lemons can last up to one year. Once fully fermented, 
the lemon peel becomes soft, aromatic, and loses its harsh sourness. These salted lemons are not 
only delicious, but also beneficial for health, digestion, and especially helpful for 
older adults, those with weak immunity, or anyone prone to coughs and sore throats. Give 
this method a try. You'll find it amazingly tasty. First, prepare a clean cutting board and a sharp knife. Place a lemon on the board. 
Then, cut it in half crosswise. Once cut, squeeze out the juice from each half. Repeat this process with all 
the lemons you want to preserve. Next, take an ice cube tray and 
gently pour the lemon juice into each compartment. This is a super 
convenient way to store lemon juice, especially for those who often make hot 
tea, honey lemon drinks, or detox beverages. Then, place the tray in the 
freezer. Whenever you need it, simply take out one frozen lemon juice cube, put it into a cup, add some warm water 
and a spoonful of honey. Stir well, and you'll instantly have a warm 
honey lemon drink. Delicious and great for your health. By following 
this method, you'll save a lot of time. Next, let's take a look at tip number five. 
This time, prepare a ziploc bag. Place the lemons inside one by one, arranging them neatly 
so they don't pile up on top of each other. If you have a lot of lemons, divide them into several 
smaller bags instead of putting them all into one. This makes it easier to take out what you 
need and ensures the lemons freeze evenly. Next, use a straw to suck out the excess air inside 
the bag. Once the air is removed, simply seal the bag tightly. After that, place the bag in the 
freezer. With this method, lemons can be preserved for up to 6 months. Have you ever tried any of 
these five methods? Which one do you think works best for you? Leave me a comment and let me know. 
That's it for today's video. I hope these little tips help you store your lemons more effectively 
and save time in the kitchen every day. If you found this video helpful, don't forget to hit 
the like button, share it with your friends, and subscribe to the channel so you won't miss out on 
more simple tips and tricks I'll be sharing soon. Thank you so much for sticking with me all the 
way to the end. I'll see you in the next videos. Wishing you a happy, easygoing day filled with 
positive energy. Goodbye and see you again soon.

of lemons you want to store. Gently arrange 
the lemons inside, either standing upright or

lying sideways, depending on the jar's shape, 
to save space. Be careful not to pack them too

tightly or bump them against one another. 
Since scratches or bruises on the skin can

affect both quality and shelf life. Next, 
take a large bowl, pour in about one quart,

one liter of clean water, then add 2 tablesp 
of salt and stir until it dissolves completely.

Pour this brine into the jar until 
all the lemons are fully submerged.

Finally, seal the jar tightly and keep it in 
a cool, airy place away from direct sunlight.

With this method, your lemons will stay fresh, 
juicy, and flavorful for two to three months.

Give it a try. You'll be pleasantly surprised 
by how well it works. Tip number two helps keep

lemons fresh without using water. First, make sure 
your lemons are thoroughly washed and completely

dried to prevent mold during storage. Next, 
prepare a piece of plastic wrap large enough

to cover one lemon. Place the lemon in the 
center of the wrap. Then, gently roll it up,

pressing the wrap tightly against the peel 
to minimize any air from getting inside.
Repeat this step with the rest of the lemons.

Once all the lemons are wrapped, neatly 
arrange them in a food grade plastic container.

Close the lid and place the container 
in the refrigerator's crisper drawer,

the ideal spot with stable humidity and 
temperature to keep lemons fresh. This

method keeps lemons juicy and fresh for about 2 
3 weeks. Plastic wrap also prevents the peel from

drying out, shriveling, or turning bitter 
from prolonged air exposure. In addition,

you can also try tip number three to 
keep lemons fresh for much longer. First,

prepare a clean cutting board and place a lemon on 
it. Use a knife to make an X-shaped cut on the top

of the lemon, but be careful not to cut all the 
way through. The bottom should still stay intact.
Repeat this step with the rest of your lemons.

After scoring, sprinkle about 1 tsp 
of salt into the slits of each lemon.

Next, place the lemons into a clean, dry glass 
jar. As you add each lemon, press down lightly so

that some of the salt falls to the bottom of the 
jar. This helps the salt distribute more evenly,

which supports a faster and more consistent 
fermentation process. Continue layering until

the jar is nearly full. At this point, you can 
add a few whole black pepperc corns or slices

of fresh ginger to enhance the flavor, making 
the preserved lemons more fragrant and rich.

Finally, pour in filtered water until the lemons 
are fully submerged. Then, seal the jar tightly.

Leave the jar at room temperature for 3 4 weeks 
to ferment, shaking or turning it occasionally to

redistribute the salt. After fermentation, store 
the jar in the refrigerator. The preserved lemons

can last up to one year. Once fully fermented, 
the lemon peel becomes soft, aromatic, and loses

its harsh sourness. These salted lemons are not 
only delicious, but also beneficial for health,

digestion, and especially helpful for 
older adults, those with weak immunity,

or anyone prone to coughs and sore throats. Give 
this method a try. You'll find it amazingly tasty.
First, prepare a clean cutting board and a sharp

knife. Place a lemon on the board. 
Then, cut it in half crosswise.
Once cut, squeeze out the juice from each

half. Repeat this process with all 
the lemons you want to preserve.

Next, take an ice cube tray and 
gently pour the lemon juice into

each compartment. This is a super 
convenient way to store lemon juice,

especially for those who often make hot 
tea, honey lemon drinks, or detox beverages.

Then, place the tray in the 
freezer. Whenever you need it,
simply take out one frozen lemon juice cube,

put it into a cup, add some warm water 
and a spoonful of honey. Stir well,

and you'll instantly have a warm 
honey lemon drink. Delicious and

great for your health. By following 
this method, you'll save a lot of time.

Next, let's take a look at tip number five. 
This time, prepare a ziploc bag. Place the

lemons inside one by one, arranging them neatly 
so they don't pile up on top of each other. If you

have a lot of lemons, divide them into several 
smaller bags instead of putting them all into

one. This makes it easier to take out what you 
need and ensures the lemons freeze evenly. Next,

use a straw to suck out the excess air inside 
the bag. Once the air is removed, simply seal

the bag tightly. After that, place the bag in the 
freezer. With this method, lemons can be preserved

for up to 6 months. Have you ever tried any of 
these five methods? Which one do you think works

best for you? Leave me a comment and let me know. 
That's it for today's video. I hope these little

tips help you store your lemons more effectively 
and save time in the kitchen every day. If you

found this video helpful, don't forget to hit 
the like button, share it with your friends, and

subscribe to the channel so you won't miss out on 
more simple tips and tricks I'll be sharing soon.

Thank you so much for sticking with me all the 
way to the end. I'll see you in the next videos.

Wishing you a happy, easygoing day filled with 
positive energy. Goodbye and see you again soon.

videos.title: CANNING FRESH LEMON JUICE

transcript.fullText: welcome back guys today I'm going to be canning lemon juice from our homegrown lemons now this tree produces a lot of lemons for us every year but there's so many on the ground we had some strong winds last night so I thought I'd get out here and uh pick them now most of them are on the top so I'm gonna have to get a ladder and pick those up there's some really big ones up the top too so we've got most of the ones down the bottom and so while it's not raining so I'm going to get the ladder out and I'll take you through to the next step well I've got a few squeezed and I've still got a lot more to do so I think I've done about a bowl and what I'm doing is just cutting them in half and just using this ordinary old-fashioned orange and lemon squeezer and then I'm pouring it into a sieve that's going to collect all the seeds and the pulp I don't want to put the pulp in some people say that with the pulp in it it tends to make it even more bitter so then I pour it into my little jug here and then into the jars now I'm going to go to about a quarter of an inch headspace so I'll need a little bit more in that one and there's three there ready to go into the canner I've already got some in the canner and I've got this big bowl to do yet so I'll just keep going but I think I'm going to have to go out and pick some more and I don't think I've got enough here even those big containers fall for a good amount in my canner so all my jars are in the canner so this is a small batch today I will be doing another batch tomorrow so I'm heating the water up and I'll wait for it to come to a rolling boil once it's come to a rolling boil I'm going to time five minutes these are half pint jars so just five minutes so I'll just pop my lid on and we'll wait for it to come up to boil so my five minutes is up and I've taken the jars out of the canner now these will sit here until tomorrow morning and as they cool down the tops will seal now these are shelf stable and they would last about from what I've read uh 18 to two years 18 months to two years so I'm sure I'll be using them well before that but I do try and do enough for one year um or there's a pop isn't that lovely and uh so tomorrow morning I'll be doing another batch but I've got something else going so I need to get these finished and I think it's a wonderful feeling that we can preserve everything that we grow I absolutely love it I hope you enjoyed this video guys thanks for watching

videos.title: I’ve Been Wasting Lemon Juice My Whole Life – You Won’t Believe How Much!

transcript.fullText: I squeezed lemons the wrong way my whole life. It turns out the typical method everyone uses is incorrect. With that method, you never get as much juice as you could. Start by taking two lemons of the same size. I will show you the proper way to squeeze them and compare both methods. The first method is the standard one you already know. Cut the lemon in half. Take a small bowl and squeeze the juice from each half. Then weigh the juice on a kitchen scale. Make sure to zero the scale first so it shows only the weight of the juice. Using the standard method, I get 23 g of juice. Now I will show you the second more effective way. Cut the lemon into several pieces. So the center has peel only on the two ends. This middle piece is key. Do not squeeze it, just twist it. Squeeze the remaining pieces into the bowl. Then weigh the juice. As you can see, there is much more juice than with the first method. Changing the way you cut and squeeze the lemon gives you noticeably more juice. Pour hot water onto the baking tray. Add a bit of vinegar. Sprinkle some baking soda. Squeeze in lemon juice and toss in the peels. Place the tray in a preheated oven for about 20 minutes. The steam will soften burnt food and grease. Finally, wipe everything down with a damp cloth. A shiny oven without using harsh chemicals. This method will make your pan look like new and in just 5 minutes. I've tried many techniques, but none worked as well as this one. Start by generously sprinkling the burnt surface with table salt. Salt works as a gentle abrasive that helps lift grime and sticks to grease. Next, grab some baking soda from your kitchen cabinet and sprinkle it over the surface. It's a natural cleaner that neutralizes odors and breaks down grease and burnt residue. Now, take the dish soap you have at home. It surfactants are great at cutting through dirt and oil. Pour it generously over the pan. Then, cover everything with layers of paper towel and pour water over it to soak the paper. Let the pan sit like this for about an hour. After that, remove the paper towel and see how the grime, burnt bits, and rust wipe away with ease. The initial heating of the pan helps activate the cleaning ingredients and softens the burnt on residue. Once the burnt layer is gone, rinse the pan with warm water. And that's it. Your pan is now clean and ready to use again. With this method, you can restore its shine without harsh chemicals or scrubbing. Try it yourself and see how easy it is to bring your pan back to life. Line a pan with aluminum foil. You're about to make something truly delicious. Start by preparing a larger pan. Take a big piece of aluminum foil, enough to cover the bottom and sides of the pan. Pour in a small bowl of popcorn kernels. Add a piece of butter for a delicious flavor. Salt the kernels to taste. And if you want to add a unique aroma, sprinkle a bit of cinnamon. Then tightly seal the edges of the foil, forming a secure pouch. Grab the edges and fold them carefully, pressing down firmly to prevent air from escaping at the sides. Now take a small knife and make a tiny hole in the center of the foil to let the steam escape. Place the pan overheat and wait a few minutes. Soon you'll hear the familiar popping sounds. That means the popcorn is popping inside the foil. With this trick, you can make popcorn without having to clean a pot or pan. Try this method and enjoy a tasty snack in just a few minutes. Delicious stove top popcorn. You can make it in just a few minutes at home. I'm sure you've never made popcorn in foil before. This trick is truly genius. First, spread foil on a pan. Make sure the foil is large enough to hold all the ingredients. Then, pour a cup of popcorn kernels on it. Add 1/3 of a cup of sugar. The sugar will make the popcorn sweet and caramelized. Now pour in 1/3 of a cup of oil to help the kernels pop evenly. Add 1 tbsp of butter. The butter will give the popcorn a wonderful rich flavor. Cover everything with another piece of foil. Fold the edges like a dumpling to create a tight seal. Make sure there are no holes where the popcorn or oil can escape. Now place the pan over medium heat. Wait for the popcorn to start popping. This will take a few minutes, so be patient. When the popping stops, carefully remove the pan from the heat. Let the foil packet cool down a bit before opening. Then, gently unfold the foil. Enjoy your hot, sweet popcorn. You've never had popcorn like this before. For this recipe, you'll need aluminum foil. Tear off two pieces of the same size. Spread one piece on the counter. Scatter the popcorn kernels on it and place a good amount of butter on top. Season with salt to your taste. Cover with the second piece of foil, pressing the edges to seal it into an envelope. Poke a hole in the center with a long toothpick. Now, heat a skillet and place the packet of kernels, butter, and salt on it. The popcorn will cook on its own. Soon you'll hear the familiar popping sound. Once it stops, remove the packet from the skillet immediately. Wait a moment, then cut open the foil packet. You'll instantly smell the buttery popcorn. This skillet method for making popcorn is easy, and the flavor has never been better. A chef who cooked at my wedding showed me this trick. Since then, I deep fry with ease and always have clean pots. Start by preparing a large pot and some parchment paper. Line the pot with parchment paper, shaping it to fit snugly. Pour in the oil you'll be using and heat it as usual. Once the oil reaches the right temperature, fry your food as you normally would. After frying, let the oil cool slightly. Then, simply lift out the parchment paper along with the oil. No mess, no scrubbing. This trick has completely changed the way I cook. Stuff the popcorn into the blender. You haven't had popcorn done like this before. This quick and fun recipe involves putting popcorn in a blender. Then pour two cups of milk over it. The milk will greatly reduce the volume of the popcorn. So add in a bit more popcorn. After that, pour in the glass of milk again. Then blend the two ingredients. After a while, pause and add 60 g of butter. Resume blending. When the popcorn is completely blended, pour the resulting mixture through a strainer. Make it immediately into the pan. Add one cup of sugar to the pan with the mixture in it. Also, add one cup of powdered milk. Cook over low heat until the powdered milk and sugar dissolve. However, stir all the time as the mask can burn very quickly. Wait until the mixture cools down. After that, pour it into a bowl. Add the whipped cream spray to the bowl. the whole container. Take a mixer and whip the resulting paste for 5 minutes. When the whipping is done, pour it into a glass container. Put it in the freezer overnight. After this time, take it out of the refrigerator. Serve the delicious popcorn flavored ice cream with waffles and your favorite fruit. Your family will go crazy over them. I don't throw away a pan when food starts sticking to it. A forgotten method restores it to its original state in just 10 minutes. No matter the type of pan, its coating, or thickness, over time, every pan loses its non-stick layer. Even expensive models aren't fully resistant, though their protection lasts a bit longer. I used to give up on pans that started sticking until I discovered a simple method passed down for our mothers and grandmothers. This technique works for both classic cast iron pans and modern ones. First, thoroughly wash the pan with baking soda to remove grease. Then, dry it and heat it on the stove. Sprinkle salt evenly across the warm surface, creating a uniform layer. Heat the salt over low heat, stirring for about 20 minutes. After that, discard the salt, wash off any residue, and move on to the next step. Apply oil to the surface using a soaked napkin or paper towel to coat the pan. Then heat the pan again and pour in a little oil, keeping it on the heat for 3 minutes. Finally, wipe off any excess oil with a paper towel. To test if the method worked, add some oil and fry an egg. If nothing sticks, the pan is good as new and ready to use again.

videos.title: Drink Baking Soda and Lemon Juice Every Morning, THIS Will Happen To Your Body!

transcript.fullText: baking soda and lemon juice can provide numerous health benefits for those individuals who consume this mixture consistently due to their medicinal properties you don't need a lot of these two ingredients to achieve their health promoting abilities simply combine one teaspoon of baking soda with the juice from half a lemon to a glass of water and try to consume in the mornings for best results consult with your medical practitioner before consuming this concoction to make sure it is right for you now let's discuss some of the amazing health benefits of drinking baking soda and lemon juice regularly first drinking baking soda and lemon juice can help to improve digestion while reducing symptoms have been digestion this includes bloating cramping gas and heartburn baking soda and lemon juice can also help to reduce inflammation in the gut again reducing indigestion symptoms and promoting the health of the gut and immune system as well lemon juice helps the liver to produce more bile which helps to keep the food we consume moving through the gastrointestinal tract while also increasing their treant absorption drinking this mixture can also help with weight loss and proper weight management studies have found that baking soda can specifically help enhance your workouts by providing more energy and also boosting metabolism baking soda can also help to reduce lactic acid buildup in the muscles during workouts which has the ability to both enhance your athletic performance while delaying fatigue lemon water can also help with weight loss lemons contain a fiber called pectin which can help us to feel more full longer and feel more satiated as well this drink can help to keep us properly hydrated consuming baking soda and lemon juice can also help to cleanse our entire body and improve the overall health of our kidneys baking soda can specifically help to improve kidney function since it is an alkaline substance baking soda can help to keep pH levels balanced while also removing acid and toxins from the body lemon juice also has tremendous cleansing properties it is a mild diuretic which helps with the elimination of toxins and other unwanted compounds from the body lemon water can also help to reduce stress placed on the kidneys promoting their health while detoxing our entire body this mixture is also great for the overall health of our skin and boosting our immune system baking soda can help to remove unwanted toxins from the body that can cause damage to the skin and result in signs of premature aging it can also help to reduce inflammation throughout our entire body positively affecting the skin lemon water is also great for skin health it is an amazing source of vitamin C and other imports and antioxidants that work to reduce oxidative stress and prevent free radical damage in the body the other nutrients and compounds found in lemon water can help to reduce wrinkles and promote collagen production lastly drinking baking soda and lemon water consistently can help to improve heart health a number of different ways studies have shown that consuming baking soda in water can help to lower bad cholesterol levels while also improving good HDL cholesterol this helps to lower the risk of developing atherosclerosis and heart disease lemon water is also great for our heart it contains heart-healthy nutrients such as vitamin C and potassium as well as great levels of fiber the antioxidant compounds in lemon water also helped to promote cardiovascular health thank you for watching the video please like comment and share if you would like to hear about more healthy foods and food and beauty tips please subscribe to the channel have a great and healthy day
